meta.title: Growing Up With K-Pop
meta.description: A story of two friends growing up together alongside K-pop.
meta.keywords: k-pop, kpop, music, generations, genre, korea

landing.title: Growing Up With K-Pop
landing.context: Two friends share their story through generations

sections.heading: Early Childhood
sections.coverAlt: Young Minji and Eunice sitting on the floor listening to BoA on a boombox, surrounded by snacks and comfy pillows

text.value: Minji and I grew up in the late 90s in the sleepy suburbs of Detroit, Michigan. We were nine years old when we met at a Korean language school that our parents took us to on Saturday mornings. In a classroom full of kids wearing Pokémon and Dragon Ball Z t-shirts, we bonded over K-pop.

Our playdates consisted of eating Korean snacks and sitting cross-legged in her family’s living room while listening to BoA’s “No.1” on repeat. Later, when we discovered YouTube, we’d crowd around her family desktop computer and watch BoA’s music videos, this time marveling at the effortless choreography and the glamorous sets. We would try to learn the dances, rewinding the same eight-count over and over, convinced that if we just watched it one more time, we'd finally get it right. We wanted so badly to be like her.

Modern K-pop is often traced back to the 1990s, when Seo Taiji and Boys, regulars at Moon Night, a club frequented by Black American GIs in Seoul, learned and brought hip hop and New Jack Swing to Korean audiences. Their sound shook the music scene and sparked a cultural shift. As the country recovered from years of political and financial crisis, the Korean government began to see pop culture, including music, television, and film, as a strategic export and form of soft power.

Of course, we didn’t know any of that at the time. We didn’t call it “K-Pop”; it was just our everyday life. Before immigrating to Detroit, my parents burnt CDs with their favorite Korean songs to bring along. We would try to sing and rap along to Seo Taiji’s 난 알아요 (I Know) in the car, finding comfort in Korean words blasting in our ears as we drove through the Michigan suburbs.

value.text: Then there were the Korean church parties, where there was always the smell of bulgogi and japchae in the air. After everyone had dinner, the adults would bring out a bulky, personal karaoke machine that someone had carried all the way from Korea. They'd sing songs from the eighties and nineties, mostly Korean ballads, but also first generation K-pop too. Music was central to our lives, and our childhood drummed to this steady beat until 2006, when Minji’s family left for California and I moved to Korea the following year.
value.speaker: eunice

text.value: My new school sat four miles from LA’s Koreatown and was predominantly Korean–a stark contrast from the majority White neighborhood I was coming from. I struggled at first to adjust to the new cultural expectations and norms, to catch jokes and conversations that flew over my head before I could make sense of them.

At recess, my classmates would talk about a K-pop group called Super Junior. At first, I only pretended I knew and liked “SuJu” to fit in. But one day after school, a friend pulled up a music video on her computer: Super Junior’s newest title track, U.

value.text: I still remember how my ears perked up at the strum of the guitar intro, how my head bopped involuntarily as the 11 members popped and locked perfectly to the beat. I ran home that day and immediately googled how to torrent music so that I could add it to my mp3 player, giddy at the thought of playing it at lunch the next day. I was officially a fan.
value.speaker: minji

text.value: Around that time, I also discovered Super Junior and felt, for the first time, the peculiar intensity of a boy band obsession. I bought their album Twins and found inside the CD a booklet with each member's face staring back at me, names printed underneath like a roster you were supposed to memorize. We didn’t call it “bias” back then - that language hadn’t reached us. But we understood, somehow, that you were meant to choose. Mine was Kibum.

By then, Minji and I had become penpals, keeping in touch over email. One day she wrote that she was an ELF (“Everlasting Friends”, the official Super Junior fandom), and I couldn’t believe it! It was thrilling to realize that we were both caught up in the same thing, in completely different places, and it became another thread holding our friendship together.

value.text: But as the years went by, our tastes started to change. By middle school, we were fangirling over hot new groups like Girls’ Generation, Wonder Girls, and BIGBANG.
value.speaker: minji

value.text: These legendary acts were capturing the hearts of the Korean general public and taking the Hallyu Wave (Korea’s global pop culture boom) all across Asia and into immigrant communities around the world. After my family moved back to Michigan, my high school’s Asian Pacific American Club incorporated K-pop into our yearly cultural show performance. I couldn’t believe that I was learning the choreography for SHINee’s “Replay” with my non-Korean friends, performing in front of an auditorium full of non-Korean classmates. While most of my classmates couldn’t point to Korea on a map, my small but tight-knit community of Asian immigrant kids were streaming K-pop videos on YouTube together after school.
value.speaker: minji

text.value: In 2012, Psy’s Gangnam Style became the first YouTube video to hit one billion videos. At its peak, Gangnam Style was generating over 11 million views per day, and prompting YouTube to upgrade its view counter system, which had maxed out at 2,147,483,647 views – a limit based on 32-bit integer coding. Suddenly, Psy was everywhere; for many people around the world, it was their first exposure to K-pop.

I remember watching Psy teach the Gangnam Style dance to Britney Spears on Ellen and getting chills. There was something thrilling about watching the world finally catch up to what we already knew. Korean pop wasn’t just “foreign.” It was fun, silly, catchy, and unmissable.

value.title: Psy’s “Gangnam Style” was the first music video to hit 1 billion views
value.subtitle: Every music video on YouTube that hit 1 billion views by 2016
value.figcaption: Line chart of YouTube music video views over time, showing how Gangnam Style reached 1 billion views in 158 days

value.text: We were living through the “Golden Age of K-Pop” from opposite sides of the world – one in the homeland, the other in the diaspora. Though we were too young to understand the forces driving this wave, the soft power that South Korea was amassing via these cultural exports, we were authentic participants of the buzz and excitement.
value.speaker: minji

text.value: Until around 2012, K-pop expanded at a pace that felt possible to keep up with. The industry was carefully managed by a handful of labels, and only a few groups debuted each year. From 1992 to 2002, often referred to as K-pop's first generation, just 33 groups debuted.

The second generation of K-pop (roughly 2003-2011) marked a shift from the industry's earlier restraint. 81 new acts emerged in just over 9 years, including generation-defining groups like Girls' Generation and BIGBANG. These artists pushed K-pop beyond domestic borders and into broader regional consciousness.

value.title: Debuts by Generation, 1992–2025
value.figcaption: K-pop groups, represented as circles, grouped by the generation they debuted in, showing the uptick of debuts in the 3rd and 4th generation
value.source: Crowdsourced from kprofiles.com and fact-checked by the community. There may be minor omissions throughout the years.

text.value: What had once been a carefully gated system became a full-blown explosion in K-pop’s third generation (roughly 2012–2017) - a period that coincided, for Minji and me, with the start of college. In just six years, 327 groups debuted, and the annual numbers rose at a staggering pace: 40 new acts in 2012, 60 by 2014, and 86 in 2017.

This surge unfolded alongside the global breakthroughs of groups like BTS and EXO, whose success reframed international popularity from a long-term aspiration into an immediate industry mandate. Agencies large and small rushed to enter the market, chasing viral potential and overseas fandoms.

value.text: I must have felt this on some level, because the early 2010s was when my attachment to K-pop began to fade. By the time I returned to the U.S. for college in 2014, my attention had already started to drift elsewhere - mostly toward Chicago, a city alive with its own rising artists and scenes. Louis the Child had released “It’s Strange” in 2015, and Chance the Rapper had come out with “Coloring Book” in 2016. These albums and other releases formed the soundtrack of my college years.
value.speaker: eunice

value.text: But even as my tastes changed, I could tell K-pop was gaining speed. My friends were saving money to go to KCON, a global K-culture festival, and I started to hear Korean songs play in the mall and on the radio. And if it wasn’t K-pop, I noticed Korea’s influence in other ways, like skincare routines, mukbang videos, and K-dramas.
value.speaker: eunice

value.text: Of course, with greater visibility came fiercer competition. The market was saturated, and the pressure to stand out grew. Idol groups and agencies had to rethink their strategies – instead of having promotion cycles based around Korean TV appearances, groups started interacting with fans year round on social media platforms like YouTube and VLive, a Korean streaming service that allowed celebrities to conduct live chat sessions with their fans.
value.speaker: minji

value.text: The surge of K-pop content, paired with a now thriving social media culture, created the perfect conditions for K-pop fandoms to grow beyond continental lines and form lives of their own. Like in Eunice’s Chicago, Korean culture was thriving at my Michigan college, where I joined a Korean-American cover band. My new friends started stanning iconic groups like Red Velvet and BLACKPINK, and I found BTS. I dug through their discography–over 200 songs at the time–and made a group chat with fellow members of the BTS ARMY that’s still active today. Together, we’d count down the days until the next “comeback” (K-pop language for a release) and undergo the impossible challenge of getting concert tickets, even making plans to travel across the country just to see them on tour. Once again, stanning a K-pop group gave me a lifelong community.
value.speaker: minji

value.text: After graduating college, I moved to New York, where I was once again in a big new city with more access to Korean culture. I spent many weekends at Koreatown pochas (street-style bars with food) where they would play the newest K-pop music videos. When 2 members of the girl group (G)I-DLE (now i-dle) participated in the virtual group K/DA for the game League of Legends, and SM powerhouse group Aespa debuted with an AI and multiverse concept, I knew we had entered a new era. While these innovations felt foreign at first, I was intrigued by the new soundscapes and fresh performances these 4th gen groups were bringing to the scene.
value.speaker: minji

images.alt: The members of the South Korean girl group aespa

value.text: Not long after, the world was shut down by a global pandemic. Hundreds of planned K-pop concerts were cancelled, and groups started releasing more online content than ever to continue connecting with fans.
value.speaker: minji

value.text: On August 20, 2020, BTS dropped Dynamite - their first English single and the first K-pop song to debut at number 1 on the Billboard Hot 100. My ARMY friends stayed up until midnight for the premiere, texting reactions in real time. We watched ARMY social media band together to break multiple records: most viewers for a YouTube Premiere, most viewed YouTube video in 24 hours, most weeks on the US Hot 100 by a K-pop track, and more.
value.speaker: minji

value.title: Nine of the Top 10 Biggest 24 Hour Music Debuts on YouTube of All-Time are from K-Pop Artists
value.figcaption: Table of top 10 YouTube music videos to receive the most views within 24 hours of debuting, which are almost all from K-pop artists

value.text: This active social media interaction was a common phenomenon among K-pop fandoms during this time. Just 2 months before Dynamite, How You Like That had broken the same YouTube records that Dynamite did. And with the popularization of TikTok around the globe, the newest 4th gen groups were able to grow massive fandoms even without having yet performed in front of a live audience.
value.speaker: eunice

text.value: Once quarantine policies were lifted, groups were eager to greet their fans around the world. All of a sudden, it felt like there was a K-pop concert to attend every other month – perhaps because I was now following multiple groups, but also because there was a true boom in US and world tours: In 2018, there were 93 US concerts by 15 unique K-pop acts. In 2022, there were 420 shows by 48 K-pop acts.
Growing up, I couldn’t imagine attending a K-pop concert in the States - now, I’m one seat in a stadium of diverse fans screaming perfect Korean lyrics.

value.title: The Increase of U.S. K-Pop Concerts After the Pandemic
value.figcaption: Line chart of the number of K-pop concerts in the U.S. between 2016 and 2025, showing a dramatic increase around 2022
value.source: Crowdsourced from r/kpop. and fact-checked by the community. There may be minor omissions throughout the years.

value.text: A few summers ago, I visited Minji in New York - our first time seeing each other since the pandemic. It didn’t take long for K-pop to come up, but the conversation felt different this time. We talked about the quiet and powerful ways K-culture and K-pop had gone mainstream. We compared notes on how we’d each kept up over the years–I'd drifted back toward the familiar sounds of 2NE1 and BIGBANG, while Minji had followed the new generations.
value.speaker: eunice

images.alt: The 3 members of HUNTR/X from K-Pop Demon Hunters

text.value: Despite its growth over the four generations, I still thought of K-pop as a relatively niche interest shared with fellow Korean-Americans and those in the know. But more recently, I’ve felt it grow beyond our intimate community and surface in places I don’t expect. I’ve seen weekly ads for local K-pop club nights, where DJs mix Top 40 pop songs with 5th gen hits like Hearts2Hearts’ FOCUS. I’ve passed by students filming K-pop dance covers in Manhattan, Rome, and Istanbul. I watched KPop Demon Hunters win a Grammy and heard coworkers’ children playing the soundtrack in the background of Zoom calls.

value.text: At the same time, we have the new excitement of seeing one of our oldest passions become so well-known and well-loved by new generations. We pass students filming K-Pop covers in Times Square and Golden Gate Park. We hear coworkers’ children playing KPop Demon Hunters in the background on calls. We see it passing the stage of sensationalism, becoming a given on the charts.
value.speaker: minji

text.value: Through all of this growth and change, we had each other to witness it with. That was its own kind of gift–having someone who had been there from the beginning, who remembered what it felt like before it was the world's. To us, the legacy of K-pop isn't in the chart numbers or streaming views but in what it gave us: the chance to see our language, culture, and passions reflected back at us on a screen.
Now the world is watching too.

Written by Minji Kim & Eunice Lee with Caitlyn Ralph
